topics.title: Strings & StringBuilder in Java
topics.story: Imagine you're carving words into stone tablets (String) versus writing on a whiteboard (StringBuilder). Stone tablets are permanent — once carved, you must create a new tablet to change anything. Whiteboards can be erased and rewritten freely. Java's String is the immutable stone tablet stored in a special pool; StringBuilder is your whiteboard for building strings efficiently. Understanding when to use each can be the difference between O(n²) and O(n) code.
topics.analogy: A String is like a published book — immutable, can be referenced by many, stored in a library (String pool). StringBuilder is a Word document you're editing — mutable, temporary, efficient for changes, but meant to produce a final String when done.
topics.memoryAnchor: PILL — Pool (string literal pool), Immutable, Literal comparison pitfall, Length is a method (not a property). Remember PILL when working with Java Strings.
topics.firstPrinciples: String is backed by a char[] (or byte[] in Java 9+ with compact strings). Immutability means every 'modification' returns a new String object. The JVM maintains a String pool (interned strings) in the heap's Metaspace. `s1 == s2` compares references, not content — always use `.equals()`. `String + String` in a loop compiles to new StringBuilder per iteration (before Java 9), causing O(n²) allocations. Java 9+ uses invokedynamic/StringConcatFactory which is smarter but still prefer explicit StringBuilder for hot loops.
topics.bruteForce: Use String concatenation in loops (functional but slow). Use indexOf/substring for searching/parsing (readable). Use Character class methods for char classification. Start with String methods before reaching for regex.
topics.optimizedApproach: Use StringBuilder for building strings in loops. Use String.format() or formatted() for templates. For pattern matching, compile Pattern once and reuse Matcher. Use char[] directly for performance-critical char-by-char processing. Use String.chars() stream for functional char processing.
topics.whenToUse: String: immutable content that's passed around, used as Map key, compared, stored. StringBuilder: building strings in loops, parsing, constructing SQL/JSON/HTML. StringBuffer: thread-safe StringBuilder (rare — prefer StringBuilder + external synchronization). String.format: readable templates. char[]: passwords (can be wiped), performance char processing.

examples.title: String Methods Reference
examples.tracerSteps: [{"step":1,"line":4,"phase":"DECLARE","variables":{"s":"  Hello, World!  "},"description":"String literal stored in pool"},{"step":2,"line":7,"phase":"CALL","variables":{"result":17},"description":"length() = 17"},{"step":3,"line":8,"phase":"CALL","variables":{"result":"Hello, World!"},"description":"trim() removes leading/trailing spaces"},{"step":4,"line":16,"phase":"CALL","variables":{"result":5},"description":"indexOf('o') = 5"},{"step":5,"line":27,"phase":"DECLARE","variables":{"a":"apple","b":"apple"},"description":"both point to same pool object"},{"step":6,"line":29,"phase":"CONDITION_TRUE","variables":{"a==b":true},"description":"same reference from pool"},{"step":7,"line":30,"phase":"CONDITION_FALSE","variables":{"a==c":false},"description":"new String creates heap object, different reference"},{"step":8,"line":31,"phase":"CONDITION_TRUE","variables":{"a.equals(c)":true},"description":"equals compares content, not reference"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A[String literal] --> B[String Pool lookup]
    B -- exists --> C[return pooled reference]
    B -- new --> D[create in pool, return reference]
    E[new String-s-] --> F[heap object, NOT pooled]
    G[==] --> H[reference comparison]
    I[.equals] --> J[content comparison]
    K[.intern] --> L[force pool storage]
examples.pseudocode:
s = "  Hello, World!  "
s.trim() → removes ASCII whitespace
s.strip() → removes Unicode whitespace
csv.split(",") → String[] of tokens
String.join("-", arr) → concatenated with separator
a == b → reference equality (avoid for Strings)
a.equals(b) → content equality (always use this)
examples.explanation: Key pitfall: `==` compares references. Two String literals with same content share the same pool reference, but `new String(...)` always creates a new heap object. Always use .equals() for String comparison. Java 11 added isBlank() (checks whitespace only), strip() (Unicode-aware trim), lines(), repeat(). substring() is O(1) in Java 7+ (no longer copies char[]).
examples.realWorldUse: String parsing in REST controllers (@RequestParam), configuration reading (application.properties), building SQL queries (use PreparedStatement!), logging messages, JSON key access.

examples.title: StringBuilder — Efficient String Building
examples.tracerSteps: [{"step":1,"line":4,"phase":"DECLARE","variables":{"bad":""},"description":"empty String"},{"step":2,"line":6,"phase":"LOOP_ITER","variables":{"i":0},"description":"bad += '0, ' creates new String object"},{"step":3,"line":6,"phase":"LOOP_ITER","variables":{"i":4},"description":"5 new String objects created total — O(n²)"},{"step":4,"line":11,"phase":"DECLARE","variables":{"sb":"StringBuilder()"},"description":"internal char[16] buffer"},{"step":5,"line":13,"phase":"LOOP_ITER","variables":{"i":0},"description":"append '0' then append ', '"},{"step":6,"line":16,"phase":"ASSIGN","variables":{"sb":"0, 1, 2, 3, 4, "},"description":"setLength trims trailing 2 chars"},{"step":7,"line":20,"phase":"CALL","variables":{"s":"Hello"},"description":"append → insert → delete → replace chain"},{"step":8,"line":26,"phase":"CALL","variables":{"s":"avaJ olleH"},"description":"reverse() reverses in-place"},{"step":9,"line":27,"phase":"CALL","variables":{"s":"vaJ olleH"},"description":"deleteCharAt(0) removes first char"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A[String concat in loop] --> B[new String object per iteration]
    B --> C[O n² allocations]
    D[StringBuilder.append] --> E[write to internal buffer]
    E --> F{buffer full?}
    F -- yes --> G[grow buffer 2x]
    F -- no --> H[continue]
    G --> H
    H --> I[toString → final String copy]
examples.pseudocode:
BAD: s = s + x  // O(n) copy each time
GOOD: sb.append(x)  // O(1) amortized

StringBuilder capacity grows 2x when full
setLength(n) truncates or pads with null chars
reverse() reverses all chars in-place
insert(idx, str) shifts existing chars right
delete(from, to) shifts chars left
examples.explanation: StringBuilder uses an internal char[] that doubles when capacity is exceeded (amortized O(1) append). String concatenation in loops using + is O(n²) because each + creates a new String. StringBuilder.toString() creates one final copy. setLength(sb.length()-2) is the clean way to remove a trailing separator after a loop.
examples.realWorldUse: Building JSON strings, HTML generation, constructing SQL queries dynamically, log message formatting, reversing strings in-place.

examples.title: String Searching — KMP Algorithm
examples.tracerSteps: [{"step":1,"line":4,"phase":"DECLARE","variables":{"pattern":"aaab","lps":"[0,0,0,0]"},"description":"build LPS for 'aaab'"},{"step":2,"line":7,"phase":"LOOP_ITER","variables":{"i":1,"len":0},"description":"pattern[1]='a' == pattern[0]='a' → lps[1]=1, len=1"},{"step":3,"line":7,"phase":"LOOP_ITER","variables":{"i":2,"len":1},"description":"pattern[2]='a' == pattern[1]='a' → lps[2]=2, len=2"},{"step":4,"line":7,"phase":"LOOP_ITER","variables":{"i":3,"len":2},"description":"pattern[3]='b' != pattern[2]='a' → len=lps[1]=1"},{"step":5,"line":7,"phase":"LOOP_ITER","variables":{"i":3,"len":1},"description":"'b' != 'a' → len=lps[0]=0"},{"step":6,"line":7,"phase":"LOOP_ITER","variables":{"i":3,"len":0},"description":"'b' != 'a' (len=0) → lps[3]=0, i=4"},{"step":7,"line":20,"phase":"LOOP_START","variables":{"i":0,"j":0},"description":"search: text='aaabaaab', pattern='aaab'"},{"step":8,"line":22,"phase":"LOOP_ITER","variables":{"i":4,"j":4},"description":"j==m=4 → found at i-j=0"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A[build LPS table O-m] --> B[search phase O-n]
    B --> C{text-i == pattern-j?}
    C -- yes --> D[i++, j++]
    D --> E{j == m?}
    E -- yes --> F[FOUND at i-j]
    E -- no --> C
    C -- no --> G{j != 0?}
    G -- yes --> H[j = lps-j-1, skip matched prefix]
    G -- no --> I[i++]
    H --> C
    I --> C
examples.pseudocode:
BUILD LPS(pattern):
  lps[0] = 0
  len=0, i=1
  WHILE i < m:
    IF pattern[i] == pattern[len]: lps[i++] = ++len
    ELSE IF len != 0: len = lps[len-1]
    ELSE: lps[i++] = 0

SEARCH(text, pattern):
  WHILE i < n:
    IF text[i] == pattern[j]: i++, j++
    IF j == m: FOUND
    ELSE IF mismatch: j = (j!=0) ? lps[j-1] : i++
examples.explanation: KMP builds a 'failure function' (LPS: Longest Proper Prefix = Suffix) that allows the pattern to slide intelligently on mismatch, avoiding re-examination of already-matched characters. O(n+m) total vs O(n*m) naive. The LPS tells us: 'if we fail at position j, we already know the first lps[j-1] characters match'.
examples.realWorldUse: Text editors (find/replace), log parsing, DNA sequence matching, network intrusion detection (pattern matching in packet payloads), plagiarism detection.

examples.title: Regular Expressions — Email and Phone Validation
examples.tracerSteps: [{"step":1,"line":6,"phase":"DECLARE","variables":{"EMAIL_PATTERN":"compiled regex"},"description":"Pattern.compile once at class load"},{"step":2,"line":13,"phase":"CALL","variables":{"email":"user@example.com"},"description":"matcher.matches() checks full string"},{"step":3,"line":13,"phase":"RETURN","variables":{"result":true},"description":"valid email"},{"step":4,"line":18,"phase":"CALL","variables":{"m":"Matcher"},"description":"m.find() scans for next match in text"},{"step":5,"line":19,"phase":"LOOP_ITER","variables":{"match":"admin@site.org"},"description":"first match found"},{"step":6,"line":19,"phase":"LOOP_ITER","variables":{"match":"support@help.io"},"description":"second match found"},{"step":7,"line":33,"phase":"LOOP_ITER","variables":{"group1":"2024","group2":"12","group3":"25"},"description":"capture groups extracted"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A[Pattern.compile regex] --> B[Pattern object cached]
    B --> C[pattern.matcher-text]
    C --> D{matches or find?}
    D -- matches --> E[full string match]
    D -- find --> F[scan for next occurrence]
    F --> G[m.group - whole match]
    F --> H[m.group-n - capture group n]
    G --> F
examples.pseudocode:
// Compile once (expensive)
Pattern p = Pattern.compile(regex)

// Use many times (cheap)
Matcher m = p.matcher(input)
m.matches()  → full string match
m.find()     → next occurrence
m.group()    → matched text
m.group(n)   → nth capture group
m.start()    → start index of match
m.end()      → end index of match
examples.explanation: Pattern.compile() is expensive (NFA construction) — always compile as a static final constant. matches() checks the entire string; find() scans for occurrences. Capture groups use parentheses in the pattern; group(1) returns the first group. Use `\\d` in Java strings (double backslash: one for Java string, one for regex).
examples.realWorldUse: Form validation in web frameworks, log parsing (extracting timestamps/IPs), URL routing, data extraction from unstructured text. Spring's @PathVariable uses regex patterns. Logback and Log4j use regex for log filtering.

examples.title: String Interning, Immutability and char[] for Passwords
examples.tracerSteps: [{"step":1,"line":4,"phase":"DECLARE","variables":{"a":"pool:Java"},"description":"'Java' added to string pool"},{"step":2,"line":5,"phase":"DECLARE","variables":{"b":"pool:Java"},"description":"same pool object returned"},{"step":3,"line":6,"phase":"DECLARE","variables":{"c":"heap:Java"},"description":"new heap String, not in pool"},{"step":4,"line":7,"phase":"DECLARE","variables":{"d":"pool:Java"},"description":"intern() returns pool reference"},{"step":5,"line":14,"phase":"ASSIGN","variables":{"s":"hello"},"description":"s unchanged"},{"step":6,"line":15,"phase":"ASSIGN","variables":{"upper":"HELLO"},"description":"new String object"},{"step":7,"line":25,"phase":"DECLARE","variables":{"password":"[s,e,c,r,e,t]"},"description":"char array for sensitive data"},{"step":8,"line":27,"phase":"ASSIGN","variables":{"password":"[\\0,\\0,\\0,\\0,\\0,\\0]"},"description":"zeroed out, no trace in memory"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A[String literal] --> B{in pool?}
    B -- yes --> C[return pool ref]
    B -- no --> D[add to pool, return ref]
    E[new String-s-] --> F[heap object, skip pool]
    F --> G[intern] --> C
    H[String modification] --> I[new String object]
    H --> J[original unchanged]
    K[char-password] --> L[use]
    L --> M[Arrays.fill zeros]
    M --> N[wiped from memory]
examples.pseudocode:
String pool: shared storage for literals
== → reference comparison
.equals() → content comparison
.intern() → force pool storage

Immutability:
  s.toUpperCase() → new String
  s unchanged

Password safety:
  char[] → can be zeroed
  String → stays in pool, GC timing uncertain
examples.explanation: String immutability enables safe sharing and use as HashMap keys (hashCode is stable). The String pool reduces memory for repeated literals. char[] for passwords is a security best practice: strings stay in memory until GC runs (timing unpredictable), and could appear in heap dumps. char[] can be explicitly zeroed. String.formatted() (Java 15+) is the instance-method version of String.format().
examples.realWorldUse: Strings as Map keys (always safe), HTTP headers, configuration keys. char[] for password in javax.crypto, JDBC password authentication, KeyStore APIs — these all accept char[] not String precisely for this security reason.

problems.title: Reverse a String
problems.description: Reverse a string s in-place (given as char array). Do not allocate extra space.
problems.hint1: Use two pointers starting at both ends.
problems.hint2: Swap characters at left and right, then move them toward each other.
problems.hint3: Stop when left >= right.
problems.constraints: 1 <= s.length <= 10^5. s[i] is a printable ASCII character.
problems.editorial: Classic two-pointer swap. O(n) time, O(1) space. XOR swap without temp: s[l] ^= s[r]; s[r] ^= s[l]; s[l] ^= s[r]; — valid but less readable.

problems.title: Valid Anagram
problems.description: Given two strings s and t, return true if t is an anagram of s (same characters, same frequencies, possibly different order).
problems.hint1: An anagram has the same character frequencies. Count characters in s and t.
problems.hint2: Use an int[26] array. Increment for s, decrement for t.
problems.hint3: If all values are 0 at the end, it's an anagram.
problems.constraints: 1 <= s.length, t.length <= 5*10^4. s and t consist of lowercase letters.
problems.editorial: Frequency array approach: O(n) time, O(1) space. First check lengths to early-exit. Increment for s, decrement for t — if anagram, all slots return to 0.

problems.title: Valid Palindrome
problems.description: Given a string, determine if it's a palindrome considering only alphanumeric characters and ignoring case.
problems.hint1: Use two pointers. Skip non-alphanumeric characters.
problems.hint2: Compare characters at both ends after converting to lowercase.
problems.hint3: Character.isLetterOrDigit(c) to filter, Character.toLowerCase(c) for normalization.

testCases.input: A man, a plan, a canal: Panama

problems.constraints: 1 <= s.length <= 2*10^5. s consists of printable ASCII characters.
problems.editorial: Two-pointer approach with inner while loops to skip non-alphanumeric chars. Compare lowercase. O(n) time, O(1) space. The inner while loops don't add to overall complexity — total character advances = O(n).

problems.title: Longest Common Prefix
problems.description: Find the longest common prefix string among an array of strings. Return empty string if none.
problems.hint1: Take the first string as the reference prefix.
problems.hint2: For each subsequent string, shorten the prefix until it matches the string's start.
problems.hint3: Use String.startsWith() or compare character by character.
problems.constraints: 1 <= strs.length <= 200. 0 <= strs[i].length <= 200. Lowercase letters only.
problems.editorial: Horizontal scanning: take first string as prefix, shorten it until each other string starts with it. O(S) where S = total chars. Alternative: vertical scanning (compare column by column).

problems.title: Count and Say
problems.description: The count-and-say sequence: '1' → '11' (one 1) → '21' (two 1s) → '1211' (one 2, one 1). Generate the nth term.
problems.hint1: Start with '1' and generate each subsequent term from the previous.
problems.hint2: Use a loop to count consecutive same characters.
problems.hint3: Append count + character to StringBuilder.
problems.editorial: Run-length encoding of the previous string. Nested while loop: outer advances j to next different character, inner counts the run. O(2^n) string length in worst case but bounded for n<=30.

problems.title: Roman Numeral to Integer using StringBuilder
problems.description: Build a method that takes an integer (1-3999) and converts it to a Roman numeral string using StringBuilder.
problems.hint1: Create two arrays: values[] and symbols[]. Use the same index in both.
problems.hint2: Repeatedly subtract the largest fitting value and append its symbol.
problems.hint3: Include subtractive pairs: 4→IV, 9→IX, 40→XL, etc.
problems.editorial: Greedy: always subtract the largest fitting Roman value. Include all subtractive combinations (CM, CD, XC, XL, IX, IV) in the values array so they're handled naturally. O(1) since number is bounded.

problems.title: Minimum Window Substring
problems.description: Given strings s and t, find the minimum window in s that contains all characters of t. Return empty string if none.
problems.hint1: Use a frequency map for t characters. Use a sliding window on s.
problems.hint2: Track 'formed' (how many unique chars in window match t's requirements).
problems.hint3: Shrink left when all t characters are covered; expand right otherwise.
problems.constraints: 1 <= s.length, t.length <= 10^5. s and t consist of uppercase/lowercase letters.
problems.editorial: Variable sliding window with character frequency tracking. 'formed' tracks how many distinct chars have met their required count. Use int[128] arrays (ASCII) for O(1) lookup. O(|s| + |t|) time.

problems.title: Group Anagrams
problems.description: Given an array of strings, group the anagrams together. Each group can be in any order.
problems.hint1: Anagrams have the same sorted character form (e.g., 'eat', 'tea', 'ate' all sort to 'aet').
problems.hint2: Use a HashMap where key = sorted string, value = list of anagrams.
problems.hint3: Alternative key: character frequency array converted to String.
problems.constraints: 1 <= strs.length <= 10^4. 0 <= strs[i].length <= 100. Lowercase letters.
problems.editorial: Sort each string to create a canonical key. computeIfAbsent initializes the list if absent. O(n * k log k) where k = max string length. Alternative O(n*k) key: use count array '#0a1b2c...' as string key.

problems.title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
problems.description: Find the length of the longest substring without repeating characters.
problems.hint1: Use a sliding window with a HashSet to track current window characters.
problems.hint2: Expand right when the new char is unique in the window.
problems.hint3: Shrink from left when a duplicate is found.
problems.constraints: 0 <= s.length <= 5*10^4. s consists of English letters, digits, symbols, spaces.
problems.editorial: Optimized sliding window: instead of shrinking one step at a time, jump left directly to lastSeen[c]+1 when a duplicate is found. int[128] for ASCII characters. O(n) time, O(1) space (bounded alphabet).

problems.title: String to Integer (atoi)
problems.description: Implement atoi: convert a string to a 32-bit signed integer. Handle leading whitespace, sign, overflow, and invalid characters.
problems.hint1: Step 1: skip leading whitespace. Step 2: read optional sign. Step 3: read digits.
problems.hint2: Stop on the first non-digit character.
problems.hint3: Check overflow before multiplying: if (result > (Integer.MAX_VALUE - digit) / 10) → overflow.
problems.constraints: 0 <= s.length <= 200. s consists of printable ASCII.
problems.editorial: Four phases: whitespace skip, sign, digits, stop on non-digit. Overflow check before multiplication: `result > (MAX_VALUE - digit) / 10`. This handles the boundary case where result would exceed int range. O(n) time.

problems.title: Decode String
problems.description: Given encoded string like '3[a2[c]]', decode it to 'accaccacc'. Brackets can be nested.
problems.hint1: Use two stacks: one for counts (k), one for strings built so far.
problems.hint2: On '[': push current count and current string onto stacks. On ']': pop and repeat.
problems.hint3: Use StringBuilder to build the repeated string.
problems.constraints: 1 <= s.length <= 30. s consists of lowercase letters, digits, and brackets. Guaranteed valid.
problems.editorial: Stack approach: when we see '[', save current state (count + built string). When we see ']', pop and repeat. k = k*10 + digit handles multi-digit numbers. O(maxNested * length) time.

problems.title: Word Break
problems.description: Given string s and dictionary wordDict, determine if s can be segmented into space-separated sequence of dictionary words.
problems.hint1: dp[i] = true if s[0..i-1] can be segmented.
problems.hint2: For each position i, try all j < i where dp[j] is true and s[j..i-1] is in the dictionary.
problems.hint3: Use a HashSet for O(1) dictionary lookup.
problems.constraints: 1 <= s.length <= 300. 1 <= wordDict.length <= 1000.
problems.editorial: DP: dp[i] = can we reach position i. For each i, try all j where dp[j]=true and s[j..i] is a word. O(n²) time with HashSet lookup. Break early when dp[i] is set. Optimized: only try j where s.length() <= max word length.

problems.title: Implement strStr (KMP)
problems.description: Implement strStr(haystack, needle): return index of first occurrence of needle in haystack, or -1 if not found. Must be O(n+m) — implement KMP.
problems.hint1: Build the LPS (failure function) array from the needle.
problems.hint2: Use two pointers i (haystack) and j (needle). On match advance both; on mismatch use LPS.
problems.hint3: When j == needle.length(), found at i - j.
problems.constraints: 0 <= haystack.length, needle.length <= 5*10^4.
problems.editorial: KMP in O(n+m). The LPS table allows the algorithm to skip redundant comparisons. When a mismatch occurs at needle[j], fall back to needle[lps[j-1]] — the longest proper prefix that's also a suffix. Never goes back in haystack.

problems.title: Palindrome Partitioning
problems.description: Given a string s, partition it such that every substring is a palindrome. Return all possible palindrome partitions.
problems.hint1: Use backtracking: at each position, try all substrings starting here that are palindromes.
problems.hint2: Check palindrome with a two-pointer approach.
problems.hint3: Precompute palindrome checks with DP: isPalin[i][j] = (s[i]==s[j] && isPalin[i+1][j-1]).
problems.constraints: 1 <= s.length <= 16. s consists of lowercase letters.
problems.editorial: DP precomputes all palindrome substrings in O(n²). Backtracking then only tries palindromic prefixes. The DP recurrence: isPalin[i][j] = same endpoints and inner is also palindrome (or length <= 2). Bottom-up from short to long. O(n * 2^n) time for backtracking part.

problems.title: Regular Expression Matching
problems.description: Implement regex matching with '.' (any char) and '*' (zero or more of preceding element).
problems.hint1: dp[i][j] = does s[0..i-1] match p[0..j-1].
problems.hint2: Base case: dp[0][0] = true. Empty pattern matches empty string.
problems.hint3: For p[j-1]=='*': dp[i][j] = dp[i][j-2] (zero use) OR (chars match && dp[i-1][j]).
problems.constraints: 1 <= s.length <= 20. 1 <= p.length <= 30. s contains only lowercase. p contains lowercase, '.', '*'.
problems.editorial: 2D DP: dp[i][j] = s[0..i-1] matches p[0..j-1]. For '*': zero occurrence (dp[i][j-2]) or one-more (dp[i-1][j] if chars match). The key insight: '*' must be treated with its preceding character as a unit. O(m*n) time and space.

problems.title: Longest Palindromic Substring
problems.description: Find the longest palindromic substring in a string. Return any one if multiple of same length.
problems.hint1: For each index, try to expand a palindrome centered at that index.
problems.hint2: Handle both odd-length (center = i) and even-length (center between i and i+1).
problems.hint3: Track the start and max length of the longest palindrome found.
problems.constraints: 1 <= s.length <= 1000. s consists of digits and letters.
problems.editorial: Expand-around-center: O(n²) time, O(1) space. Each center expands outward while chars match. Two expansions per center (odd + even). After expansion, l and r have moved one step too far (mismatch), so palindrome length = r - l - 1, start = l + 1. Manacher's algorithm achieves O(n) but is complex.

problems.title: Interleaving String
problems.description: Given s1, s2, s3, determine if s3 is formed by an interleaving of s1 and s2.
problems.hint1: dp[i][j] = can s3[0..i+j-1] be formed by interleaving s1[0..i-1] and s2[0..j-1].
problems.hint3: Base case: dp[0][0] = true. Initialize first row/col.
problems.constraints: 0 <= s1.length, s2.length <= 100. s3.length == s1.length + s2.length.
problems.editorial: 2D DP where dp[i][j] represents using i chars from s1 and j chars from s2 to form s3[0..i+j-1]. A char from s3 can come from s1 OR s2. O(m*n) time and space.

problems.title: Wildcard Matching
problems.description: Implement wildcard pattern matching with '?' (match any single character) and '*' (match any sequence including empty).
problems.hint1: dp[i][j] = does s[0..i-1] match p[0..j-1].
problems.hint2: For '*': dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] (use *) || dp[i][j-1] (skip *).
problems.hint3: For '?' or exact match: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1].
problems.constraints: 0 <= s.length, p.length <= 2000. s contains lowercase letters. p contains lowercase, '?', '*'.
problems.editorial: Simpler than regex DP: '*' matches empty (skip with dp[i][j-1]) or one char (use with dp[i-1][j]). '?' matches exactly one char (like exact match). Initialize first column: stars can match empty. O(m*n) time and space.

problems.title: Shortest Palindrome (Add to Front)
problems.description: Find the shortest palindrome by adding characters to the front of the string. Equivalent to finding the longest palindromic prefix.
problems.hint1: The answer is: (suffix that's not part of palindromic prefix, reversed) + s.
problems.hint2: Build the string: s + '#' + reverse(s). Compute KMP failure function on this.
problems.hint3: The last value of the failure function tells the length of the longest palindromic prefix.
problems.constraints: 0 <= s.length <= 5*10^4. s consists of lowercase letters.
problems.editorial: Key insight: the longest palindromic prefix of s has length = KMP failure function value of the last char in s+'#'+rev(s). The '#' separator prevents spurious matches. The characters not in the palindromic prefix are prepended in reverse. O(n) time using KMP.

problems.title: Edit Distance (Levenshtein)
problems.description: Given two strings word1 and word2, return the minimum number of operations (insert, delete, replace a character) to convert word1 to word2.
problems.hint1: dp[i][j] = min ops to convert word1[0..i-1] to word2[0..j-1].
problems.hint2: If chars match: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]. If not: dp[i][j] = 1 + min(replace, insert, delete).
problems.hint3: replace = dp[i-1][j-1], delete = dp[i-1][j], insert = dp[i][j-1].
problems.constraints: 0 <= word1.length, word2.length <= 500. Words consist of lowercase letters.
problems.editorial: Classic DP. dp[0][j]=j (j insertions to build word2 from empty). dp[i][0]=i (i deletions). Transition: same char → inherit diagonal. Different → 1 + best of three operations. O(m*n) time and space. Space can be reduced to O(min(m,n)) with rolling array.
